body:
### Problem

#39006 suggests upstreaming tree-sitter related data in a separate Neovim-owned plugin similar to 'nvim-lspconfig'. The difference (at least initially) is to have only "top 20" / "top X" languages to manage project complexity.

However, "tail languages" will still exist outside of a "top X". Even if the constraint is relaxed, I still have doubts that *all* languages will be upstreamed.

This creates a problem for users who want to create and maintain Neovim tree-sitter support for such "tail languages".

### Solution

Document, either here or in the upstreamed tree-sitter plugin, how to go about adding a tree-sitter support for a language.

Let's say there is a new language. Let's say it is named vim10script. My current understanding of the most realistic way of adding a tree-sitter support in Neovim for it is as follows:

- Publish a 'tree-sitter-vim10script' repo with tree-sitter parser. It may also contain reference queries (which are not immediately useful for Neovim since they are 'queries/\*.scm' and not 'queries/vim10script/\*.scm') and CI builds of specific parser versions (WASM or not).

- Publish a 'vim10script.nvim' language plugin that contains Neovim specific runtime files ('ftplugin/', 'lsp/', etc.). For a tree-sitter support it needs to:

    - Include 'queries/vim10script/*.scm' query files.
    
    - Document which source and version of a parser the query files are written for. It is then a user's task to make sure that the parser at that version is available in the Nvim session. My guess is that it will be usually done by some third-party "tree-sitter installer plugin".

      To make it easy to automate, using an existing convention of `:h treesitter-query-modeline` seems like a least friction approach. Add a `;; parser https://github.com/vim-user/tree-sitter-vim10script abcdef123` on top of the query file and it is readable for any user and any plugin. It is then a matter of "tree-sitter installer plugin" to properly make this parser at specific revision available for the user.

comment_data.body:
Copying https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39006#issuecomment-4244107036 here since it seems this may be the better place to discuss this:

> Pushing queries into the parse repositories is a good idea, but this does pose a question: what should the structure for the queries directory be, and how would that work if the parser wants to support multiple languages?
> 
> As an example, imagine https://github.com/inko-lang/tree-sitter-inko/ wants to support both Helix and Neovim, and both move to a model of pushing the queries into the parser repositories. Both these editors use different capture groups and possibly even different queries for e.g. indents. The result is that dumping queries in `queries/LANG/QUERY.scm` (e.g. `queries/inko/indents.scm`) won't work as there's no mechanism for grouping them per editor/consumer.
> 
> The structure `queries/CONSUMER/LANG/QUERY.scm` (e.g. `queries/nvim/inko/indents.scm`) wouldn't really work because the structure of `queries/nvim` isn't something you can directly add to the runtime path as it currently would expect something like `queries/nvim/queries/inko/indents.scm`.
> 
> The structure `CONSUMER/queries/LANG/QUERY.scm` (e.g. `nvim/queries/inko/indents.scm`) would be better and make it easier to add the directories to the runtime path, effectively only needing something like this:
> 
> ```
> for dir in parser-directories
>   runtime_path += dir/nvim
> ```
> 
> > It is "data-only", it doesn't have a "framework". No "indent" support. (Features requiring a framework should live in Nvim's own stdlib.)
> 
> So just so I understand this correctly, does this mean that indent support is to be moved in nvim itself, or is indent support going to be removed entirely?

Now that I think of it some more: 

Pushing queries into the parser repositories does introduce a problem: it puts the burden of maintaining queries for 15 different consumers (e.g. editors) on the parser maintainer. Using my own parser as an example again, I don't use Zed so I wouldn't be able to effectively maintain the queries for it should they also be pushed into the parser repositories.

Perhaps a better way is to do the following:

- Neovim only provides a few basic routines for downloading and installing parsers and adding query directories to the runtime path. There is no `nvim-treeconfig` or whatever repository
- Parser repositories only provide the parser code
- For each language there exists a separate nvim plugin repository that adds the usual language stuff (indentation, syntax, etc) and optionally also adds tree sitter support using the Neovim-provided foundation

So using Inko as an example, this means that you'd have to install https://github.com/inko-lang/inko.vim which would provide the necessary queries and what not. For other languages this could either go in the main plugin repository as well or one dedicated to Neovim (e.g. https://github.com/rust-lang/rust.vim is unmaintained so for Rust it would have to be a separate repository).

This approach has the following benefits:

- Neovim maintainers only need to provide some basic scaffolding/foundations for installing parsers and adding query directories and such, and maybe some foundations for e.g. indents
- Parser maintainers only need to concern themselves with the parser
- Maintainers of the integration plugin _do_ need to concern themselves with both the parser and the queries, but there's sadly no way around that given the two are tightly connected. However in exchange they don't have to depend on the other groups (e.g. they don't need to upstream query changes anywhere)

To ease the discovery process of parsers, Neovim could provide some sort of registry repository that's literally just a README with a list of integration plugins, or maybe include this for popular languages in the help files. For everything else it would be the usual process also used for discovering other third-party plugins.

> * Neovim maintainers only need to provide some basic scaffolding/foundations for installing parsers and adding query directories and such, and maybe some foundations for e.g. indents

It doesn't necessarily has to be part of Nvim itself, i.e. not as a part of `vim.treesitter`. What would be helpful is designing, agreeing, and documenting an approach for such language plugins to declare which tree-sitter parser (source+version) it is built for. Like mentioned above `;; parser <source> <revision>` modeline. Then some "tree-sitter installer plugin" (third-party or maybe upstreamed 'nvim-treeconfig') can use that information to install relevant parser version.

Another approach might be declaring in "language plugin" via https://github.com/neovim/packspec, but as a parser is not a Neovim plugin and requires some extra actions to be useful (i.e. build+generate), it doesn't look like a good fit.

@echasnovski The idea I had in mind is that an integration plugin (e.g. inko.vim in my example) does something like this:

```lua
local M = {}

function M.setup(opts)
  if opts.treesitter then
    vim.treesitter.install('inko', 'https://github.com/inko-lang/tree-sitter-inko', 'v0.6.0')
  end

  ... other stuff ...
end

return M
```

The project structure would then be something like this:

```
queries/
  inko/
    highlights.scm
    ...
lua/
  inko/
    init.lua
ftplugin/
  ...
```

You'd then set up it like so:

```lua
require('inko').setup({ treesitter = true })
```

That is: there's a setup function of sorts that installs a parser from a specific source and with a specific revision. Users won't be able to upgrade the parser unless that install line is changed, as upgrading a parser beyond what the plugin supports is almost certainly going to break things (e.g. queries).

This approach wouldn't require any mode lines or whatever because it's up to the integration plugin to decide what version it needs using regular Lua code. This way if the plugin wants to support multiple versions (e.g. so you can work with different versions of a language if that requires different syntax), it can do so without Neovim having to cater towards that (beyond allowing you to install multiple versions of the same parser, assuming that's even desired to begin with).

Indeed the `vim.treesitter.install` bit _could_ be a separate plugin similar to nvim-lspconfig, but if all it does is download and install a parser I'm not sure that would be better in the long run (though as a starting point to experiment it's probably better).

> This approach wouldn't require any mode lines or whatever because it's up to the integration plugin to decide what version it needs using regular Lua code. This way if the plugin wants to support multiple versions (e.g. so you can work with different versions of a language if that requires different syntax), it can do so without Neovim having to cater towards that (beyond allowing you to install multiple versions of the same parser, assuming that's even desired to begin with).

I am not quite against this, *but* it:

1) Requires to actually design, implement, and maintain all the code related to installing/updating/deleting tree-sitter parsers.
2) I am pretty sure has some pushback from the core.
3) Locks language plugin into how `vim.treesitter.install` does things (i.e. no third party plugin improvements). And adds a Neovim version cutoff (i.e. something like Neovim>=0.13, which is a significant cutoff in the short and middle term).

An alternative is for Neovim to document that adding at the top of the `queries/inko/highlights.scm` the following line should be enough to advertise which tree-sitter parser is required:

```scm
;; parser https://github.com/inko-lang/tree-sitter-inko v0.6.0
```

And that's it. It won't require any 'lua/inko/init.lua' file and will allow using third party tree-sitter installer plugins. At the cost of pointing to the user that they *have to* install that parser at that version.

> This way if the plugin wants to support multiple versions (e.g. so you can work with different versions of a language if that requires different syntax),

That would still require multiple query files for different versions (otherwise there is no point in multiple versions). And that is not something that is currently supported by query runtime files.

> Indeed the `vim.treesitter.install` bit _could_ be a separate plugin similar to nvim-lspconfig, but if all it does is download and install a parser I'm not sure that would be better in the long run (though as a starting point to experiment it's probably better).

Unfortunately, it is never as simple as "download and install". It will also require at least "update and delete".

Been following this and similar discussions for the last couple of days. While in an ideal world I believe there should be an determined structure for treesitter repos it seems that an easier solution would be some kind of registry for nvim where each parser has a json file, or similar, specifying the url for the repo along with the path for queries and other relevant information.

Then we could specify the path of the queries in that file, and not require a specific structure. You might also be able to handle having one repo for the parser and another for the queries.

Curious about others thoughts.

@echasnovski In your proposal, what repository would the queries be located in, the parser repository or some central nvim-treeconfig repository maintained to Neovim itself? Apologies if this is already explained somewhere and I overlooked it.

> [@echasnovski](https://github.com/echasnovski) In your proposal, what repository would the queries be located in, the parser repository or some central nvim-treeconfig repository maintained to Neovim itself?

Not parser, that is separate (as described in the [initial comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39037#issue-4261289966)). And mostly for reasons described in your initial comment here: not immediately appropriate structure for Neovim runtime files, not feasible for parser authors to maintain editor-specific queries, etc.

Queries are located in the repository that is either a "Neovim language plugin for Inko language" ('inko.nvim' in this case) or a collection of tree-sitter queries that want to include Inko (like potential 'nvim-treeconfig').

I had been working on https://github.com/neovim-treesitter/ as a proposal - but it feels like it might be beneficial to discuss here now that discussions are underway.

There are plenty of details on that org and repos - but It had been my impression that the following might be key features to make things sustainable

- queries need to know a particular parser version they're compatible with
- discovery of parser / query combos to add treesitter support for a given language would ideally be easy
- updating and installing should be easy
- some queries inherit from other languages - this needs to be carefully worked into the installer for both testing and installation (installing the language needs the dependency too to work).

nvim-treesitter did this by maintaining the queries in it's own repo and referencing compatible parsers.  This implied two things from my perspective

- large maintenance headache supporting all the languages
- a single repo which is hard to manage from a rights and permissions perspective, decreasing the ability for grammar maintainers to own the nvim query bindings

The goal of the above was to sketch out a way to distribute the ownership in two key ways:

- queries must pair with a parser, but do not have to be owned by the installer.
  - separate repos for each language allow distributed ownership of the queries allowing folks close to the grammar to maintain nvim queries
  - ideally the parser would ship with nvim queries.  I sketched out a way to do both.  You can either
    - have queries be a separate repo to the parser and allow that query repo to specify a compatible parser version
    - have queries shipped with the parser, so that paring is now owned by the parser maintainer, lock step
  - a registry of known languages which the installer can grab latest versions of, where the maintenance of the paring can (but may not be) someone other than the installer
- making all this work and be reliable is as much about convention, testing machinery and agreed approach as it is about anything super technical.  That said, there are concrete things that can be done to make externally maintained languages work better:
  - clear reusable CI/CD workflows to verify that the specification of grammar and query pair install correctly
  - corpus tests to ensure the queries actually work in neovim
  - documentation for all these
  - clear "upgrade" paths for parser maintainers who want to ship nvim queries

I thought this comment:

https://github.com/orgs/neovim-treesitter/discussions/2#discussioncomment-16528016

on the sketch was important - and I'd like to work on that.  But I'd like to do so in a way that isn't in conflict with the direction the core team wants to go.  In honesty, my optimal outcome would be contributing help to making the core efforts happen.  To that end, if any of the registry-spec, installer sketch, CI/CD above seems useful, I'd love to contribute it, or use it as part of a discussion of possible routes forward.

- Overview here: https://github.com/neovim-treesitter/treesitter-parser-registry?tab=readme-ov-file
- The schema/ spec for the registry entries is here: https://github.com/neovim-treesitter/treesitter-parser-registry/blob/main/schemas/schema.json
- proposed file layout here: https://github.com/neovim-treesitter/treesitter-parser-registry/blob/main/docs/architecture.md
- supporting CI/CD reusable workflows here: https://github.com/neovim-treesitter/.github

Couple of observations:
  
> * queries must pair with a parser, but do not have to be owned by the installer.
> ...
>     * ideally the parser would ship with nvim queries.  I sketched out a way to do both.  You can either
>       
>       * have queries be a separate repo to the parser and allow that query repo to specify a compatible parser version
>       * have queries shipped with the parser, so that paring is now owned by the parser maintainer, lock step

The "have queries be a separate repo to the parser..." is not what what I'd call "must pair with a parser". Does the latter mean more like "queries must specify parser source and version they are written for"? If yes, then we're on the same page.

The "have queries shipped with the parser" doesn't look scalable. Tree-sitter parser maintainer is not necessarily interested in Neovim as an editor, so expecting it to contain Neovim conventions may introduce friction (PRs from Neovim people convincing to adhere to its conventions).

> The "have queries be a separate repo to the parser..." is not what what I'd call "must pair with a parser". Does the latter mean more like "queries must specify parser source and version they are written for"? If yes, then we're on the same page.

yes exactly.  The version has to produce the right nodes to query - many possible parser updates may pass fixing the grammar or recognition whilst this is still the case.  Others might alter the set of nodes created and break the queries.  Ideally that upgrading to new parser versions should be easy, but you need a check CI/CD to ensure it's not broken or things get funky quick.

The separate repo things is about permissions and ownership of the queries - some grammar authors may want to ship neovim queries, others may not.  Someone who actually uses the language ideally would maintain the query set, but doesn't have to be the parser maintainer.  Permissions in a monorepo are harder than those with devolved ownership.

> The "have queries shipped with the parser" doesn't look scalable. Tree-sitter parser maintainer is not necessarily interested in Neovim as an editor, so expecting it to contain Neovim conventions may introduce friction (PRs from Neovim people convincing to adhere to its conventions).

I could see that being the case, I could also see folks who use neovim wanting to produce paired queries (I do that for zsh).  The queries don't have to be one set for all editor, they just may want to ship nvim-queries with the parser.

so to clarify the above "either"

I meant the sketch supports both possibilities (both in one repo /or/ queries-separate, version of queries specifies compatible parser version)

> Indeed the vim.treesitter.install bit could be a separate plugin similar to nvim-lspconfig, but if all it does is download and install a parser

I kindof wish we would leverage vim.pack.add for downloading and managing versions. It would still require the user to build the parser and match the versions to query issues but it would be one less thing plugins would need to implement. Then you could use the `load = false` for extra piece of mind or use true if the repository includes the queries.

> I kindof wish we would leverage vim.pack.add for downloading and managing versions.

isn't that implied if we upstream nvim-treesitter into a nvim-treeconfig repo? that will be a "plugin" and it will have tags.

labels.description: packaging and distributing Nvim to users
labels.name: distribution

title: treesitter: documentation for third-party language plugins
